statusLine: EU fuel security remains under pressure with 15 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall stocks above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
While EU-wide averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum (petrol 112.8 days, diesel 101.5 days, jet fuel 95.1 days), the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status, including diesel at just 30.2 days. Poland and Luxembourg also show critical levels across all fuel categories, with Poland's jet fuel reserves at a concerning 25.3 days.

The data shows 15 of 27 EU countries have at least one fuel type in critical status, indicating widespread strain on national reserves following recent strategic releases due to Middle East supply disruptions. Jet fuel appears most vulnerable, with 17 countries below safe levels. Germany, despite strong petrol reserves at 100.7 days, shows critical diesel and jet fuel stocks at 64.3 and 64.7 days respectively.

Brent crude's sustained elevation at $106.68 per barrel, up 47.2% since late February, continues to pressure both supply costs and stock management decisions. Price variations across member states are significant, with Netherlands paying €2.389 per liter for petrol compared to Malta's €1.34, reflecting different national taxation and supply chain factors during this period of market stress.
keyPoints: 15 of 27 EU countries have critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with Romania, Poland, and Luxembourg most severely affected across all fuel types
Jet fuel shows the greatest vulnerability with 17 countries below safe reserve levels, including major economies like Germany (64.7 days) and France (67.5 days)
Brent crude remains elevated at $106.68/barrel, representing a 47.2% increase since the February Middle East escalation began
Eastern European countries show particular strain, with Romania's diesel at just 30.2 days and Poland's jet fuel at 25.3 days
Fuel prices vary dramatically across the bloc, from Netherlands at €2.389/L to Malta at €1.34/L for petrol, reflecting different national responses to supply pressures
